Observe when Jonah prayed. When he was in trouble, under the signs of God’s displeasure against him for sin, we must pray when we are in affliction. Being kept alive by a miracle, he prayed. A sense of God's good will to us, even with our offenses, opens our lips in prayer, which were closed with the dread of wrath. Also, where did he pray? In the belly of the fish. No place is unsuitable for prayer. People may shut us out from fellowship with one another, but not from fellowship with God. To whom did he pray? To the Lord his God. This encourages even those who have turned away to return. What was his prayer like? It seems to relate his experience and reflections, then and afterwards, rather than be the form or substance of his prayer. Jonah reflects on the earnestness of his prayer, and God’s readiness to hear and answer. If we want to benefit from our troubles, we must notice the hand of God in them. He had wickedly fled from the presence of the Lord, who might justly take his Holy Spirit from him, never to visit him again. Those only are miserable whom God will no longer own and favor. But though he was troubled, yet not in despair. Jonah reflects on the favor of God to him when he sought God and trusted in him in his distress. He warns others and tells them to stay close to God. Those who forsake their own duty forsake their own mercy; those who run away from the work of their place and time run away from the comfort of it. As far as a believer copies those who observe false idols, he forsakes his own mercy and lives below his privileges. But Jonah’s experience encourages others in all ages to trust in God as the God of salvation.

Jonah's deliverance may be considered as an example of God's power over all creatures. It is also an example of God's mercy to a poor, repentant person who prays to him in distress, and as a type and figure of Christ’s resurrection. Amidst all our varying experiences and the changing scenes of life, we should look by faith, fixedly, upon our once suffering and dying, but now risen and ascended Redeemer. Let us confess our sins, consider Christ's resurrection as assurance of our own, and thankfully receive every temporal and spiritual deliverance as a pledge of our eternal redemption.
